sentences.text: OK, we were talking about the certificates.
So this-- this person, a night clerk, came to that meeting.
I explained the situation with the-- with the certificate-- the exchange.
They called it exchange certificates.
Now, they-- as I said before, the Jewish agency they could attach to the exchange Palestinian Jews, their families that were stuck in Europe.
But by the time the exchange certificates has reached these people, most of these people were not alive anymore.
And Sam [?
Maverick ?] at the Jewish Agency had the idea, let's attach just plain Jews.
Let's attach to the certificates Jews that are still alive and that can be saved.

And they decided to make a list-- what they called an exchange list-- with Zionist veterans-- these people that were active or very active in the Zionist movement before the war-- that they should be candidates for exchange instead of the other people that don't exist anymore.
And at that meeting, they formed a list.
And of course, my brother knew-- we didn't know-- we didn't really know who is still there and who was not there from the ancient veterans.
But he put our family on the list.
He put my father and family-- our name was Kapelusznik.
He put Jacob Kapelusznik and family as one unit on the list.
And he put himself on the list.
And he came home, he told us the story.
We didn't attach any importance to that story.
And later on, we forgot all about it.
Now, as-- as I said, we were in hiding.
And it was already 1944.
We had all-- we had all the holidays in hiding.
It was very difficult not to go in Yom Kippur to-- to a synagogue for-- we-- we made all the prayers that take a whole day, we made them in a half hour.
And it was Passover.
Somehow, the Judenrat had matzahs.
And we sent the woman, and she got matzahs for us on Passover.
But shortly after Passover-- and I think a few days, a week or so after Passover-- one night, it was the night of April 27, 1944.
We are awakened in the middle of the night-- maybe it was around 3 o'clock-- by tremendous banging on the outside door in the house.
We immediately knew what that means.
We knew who was there.
They didn't wait for anyone to open the door.
But they broke-- they broke the door.
They broke the front door.
They came in very-- very wild and very brutal.
They're extremely brutal from the second they came into the house.
When we heard the banging-- my sister, her husband, and myself-- we decided to try to escape.
We were in our night-- nightwear, in our pajamas.
And we got out on the roof, the three of us.
The-- the Gestapo came in.
It consisted of a officer-- it was an elderly man, he was German-- a-- two Belgian SS, who were extremely brutal.
And the fourth was sitting in the car in front of the house.
And now, the SS-- which I don't know if it was German or Belgian.
They-- We went on the roof.
We heard, from the house, screaming and beating.
They were beating up my mother.
They tore off from a-- a leg from a chair.
And they beat my mother in the face.
And they beat my father, my brother.
And the three of us were on the roof.
We were on the roof a short while.
And we heard screaming in German that they see us.
And if we don't come off the roof, they'll shoot.
And matter of fact, they did kill a few people on roofs during the time where Jews were hiding.
We had to take split-second decisions.
You go down or stay on the roof.
My brother-in-law and myself, we decided to surrender.
We came down.
My sister did not.
She stayed in her nightgown.
She hid behind the chimney.
They were screaming.
But she didn't budge.
She stayed behind the chimney.
And as we came down, they in particular started to beat me up.
The SS that was beating me was very tall.
He was like a gorilla.
He was wide and tall, a giant.
He put me-- he knocked me down to the floor.
And he was standing on my chest.
How I could withstand it, I don't know.
And why he was standing on my chest-- he was beating me.
Blood was flowing from the nose in profusion.
All my-- my shirt, everything, got-- got red, [? from ?] [? there ?] was soiled by blood.
Everything was bloody.
And I didn't know why he was beating me more than anybody else.
He kept on beating me.
And then, they asked if there is more people on the roof.
And we said no.
But they found my sister's identity card.
They were very precise.
And they were very shrewd.
And they were looking in all the corners.
It was very difficult to hide anything from them.
They found my sister's identity card.
And they knew that she was on the roof.
They looked for her all over.
They couldn't find her.
The reason they couldn't find her is, while they were taking down my brother-in-law and myself, my sister continued on the roofs towards other-- other houses.
She ran on the roofs.
And she saw a window in a-- in a-- in a roof.
And she opened the window.
And she went in into a total strange house.
And these people didn't know where from that person came.
She came from Oz or something.
And I'll come back.
They took us down.
As I said, they beat me terribly.
I don't know how I-- I withstood that beating.
And then-- and they took us all down to the car that was waiting in front of the house.
And we had an extremely short drive to the Gestapo headquarters.
It was maybe a minute or two.
Once we arrived there, they-- they put us in the cellar.
They had a cellar, and they had cells in the cellar.
It was a number of cells, maybe 10 or more facing each other.
There was a hall in the middle.
The-- that was, by itself, a torture.
Because that cell didn't have a floor.
It had-- it was slanted, like a slanted wood.
And that was used as a-- as like-- like a cage for the people that they caught.
They should sit there.
They should sleep there.
They should-- and that was, maybe, for 20, 25 people they put in a cell.
As we came into the cell, as the hours went by, more and more Jews were brought in, into the cell and filled up other cells.
Jews that were in hiding, that they caught from different places.
All the time, the cars-- the Gestapo cars were bringing in more Jews.
Our cell filled up maybe with 20, 25 people and children.
And they're screaming.
And it was extremely, extremely-- uncomfortable is not the word.
Because ?] very difficult.
And we stayed in that basement, if I remember correctly, for two days and two nights.
If someone had to go to the bathroom, you had to-- you had to knock on the gates.
There was a gate, like same way as a jail cell.
And the SS would take you to the bathroom.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To stay in that-- in that basement-- we were looking forward to be taken out and taken to Mechelen, like a-- like freedom.
Because it was extremely painful to stay in that place.
After-- after two days and they accumulated enough Jews, that is the only time and the first time that I have seen Jacques the Informer.
I looked out from the-- through the gate, and there he was.
He came to the cellar to look at the Jews.
Because there must have been some Jews that he caught.
And then they took us-- from the-- from the cellar, they took us into a truck, a-- I guess a military truck.
They were two guards with rifles and bayonets at the two corners of the truck, and also two guards with the driver in the front.
Truck was full of Jews, men, women, children that were caught recently.
And the truck proceeded to Mechelen.
When we arrived in Mechelen, there was a big door, like a gate, but, you know, in like in the ancient-- the ancient forts, with a big door.
They opened up the door wide.
And the truck rolled into the courtyard.
The procedure was, when the Jews that were caught arrived to Mechelen, everything was brutal.
So, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
They would kick people off the truck.
They would kick them in the back to get them used to brutal-- to being dealt brutally.
The first procedure was the-- on the left side of the camp.
There was the place they called the Reception of [INAUDIBLE].
That was the reception for new prisoners.
The reception was manned by prisoners, by Jewish girls that they had caught.
And they decided to give them a job in the-- in the reception.
So these girls were saved until they would decide to deport them.
Some of them were there a year or more or two years.
And so, you had to-- like, these girls were registering us, name, the-- the-- where we were caught and so forth.
I don't remember exactly.
But it was some kind of registration.
You also had to give them your identity card, your rationing card, your money, your gold if you had.
I had a gold watch.
I gave them the gold watch.
People had diamonds or jewelry.
Everything was taken away.
It was put in an envelope with the name of the person.
And at that reception, it was quite brutal.
They were beating up people.
They were kicking.
They-- they-- it was-- it was a-- a-- a torture to get through the reception.
Once you were through the reception, they let you into your place, where you-- into your quarters.
The quarters were-- they-- they-- what they called [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] means a-- a room, a ball room-- I would say, a hall.
And the hall had numbers.
This was from-- from one-- I don't remember to how much.
But I remember there was this one and there was a 13.
So there must have been, maybe, 20-- 20 halls.
Each hall could take up to 100 people.
The halls had bunks, the same way they had in Auschwitz.
You know, they had established the final solution.
They must have established how the-- the quarters for Jews should look.
It looked the same way.
It was three, one on top of the other, the wooden-- the wooden bunks with their straw back.
And they put me together with my family.
We were in hall number 13.
And it was full.
I would say there were about 100 Jews in-- in that hall.
I just want to mention a detail.
People had delusions.
Like, when I was on the truck with other Jews that I didn't know, I had never seen, I remember there was a family that they caught, name was Schubowitz.
They were apparently very wealthy Jews.
And this-- the father, Mr.
Schubowitz, told me, you know, I will be out from the camp right away.
Because I have very big connections.
I'll be out with my family.
So if you want to give me something for family that you have outside, you can give me a telephone number or something.
Because I'll be out in no time.
He was there with the wife and, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think, two beautiful daughters, a very good looking young boy.
They were very good looking and sweet people.
He told me that he has with him 600,000 francs in cash.
Anyway, he arrived in-- in together with us.
They took away the 600,000 francs.
They-- no connections of his helped.
He was deported with the next transport with his entire family.
He was in the same hall with us.
What happened the-- the next day in the-- in the hall, there was an SS, which I was-- I mention him in particular, because he was particularly brutal.
He was a Belgian SS.
His name was Van Kolh.
And we called him the Horsehead, [GERMAN].
Because he looked like a horse.
His-- he had a head like a horse head.
He was the most brutal of all the SS.
Now, Mechelen-- the commander of the camp was a German.
His name was Frank.
He was a German officer.
He was a middle-aged-- middle-aged man.
He was not the main commander.
Because the camp belonged-- as I mentioned before, there was another camp, Breendonk.
And this was a branch, really, from Breendonk.
The commander from Breendonk was a higher-ranking officer.
He was a sturmbannfuhrer.
This was a major in the SS.
And our commander was a lower SS officer.
He was obersturmfuhrer.
Which is like a lieutenant or a little more than a lieutenant.
He was assisted by another German.
His name was Boden.
He was the assistant commander of the-- of the camp.
He was also German.
They were both police officers before the war, we found out later, in Leipzig.
They joined the Nazi party.
And they became big shots in the SS.
They were the commanders.
Then, there was another German officer, which we called [?
Moshe-- ?] [?
Moshe ?] [GERMAN], Moshe the Precise, because he wanted everything to precision.
For instance, there was a law that you had to salute a German officer a number of feet away from you.
When you saw a German officer, 20 feet, you had to take off your hat and salute him in a certain manner, which they taught us.
If you didn't, he could beat you up or put you in the corner with a wait for two hours, which he did to my father.
So we called him-- he was German also.
The rest of the guards were not German.
They were Belgian SS.
Some of them were extremely brutal.
A couple of them were not.
But most of them were more brutal than the Germans.
And we were more afraid of them than of the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
The-- so I'm coming back.
So we were in hall 13.
And that was-- one day, I was in the hall, and this SS that we called the Horsehead, he came in.
And he asked-- my-- he called out my name.
And I didn't understand why they calling my name.
He said, you to the camp commandant.
The commandant is calling you.
He walked me through the courtyard.
And I-- I came in toward the commandant's office.
The commandant wasn't there.
But the assistant was, this Boden.
He was a very mean and brutal German.
The commandant was not as bad as him.
He called me in.
And he took out a letter.
And he said, this is a letter from the Gestapo headquarters in Brussels that you, he said, were on the roof and you tried to push off the roof the SS that was looking for you.
So in other words, he accused me of attempted murder of an SS, which was very bad.
Just to be a Jew was bad.
But to be a Jew that's accused of throwing off the roof an SS was very bad.
And they started beating me up, he in the front and another SS, which we call [?
Journe-- ?] I think that was his real name.
And he was beating me in the back.
This [?
Journe ?] was a very strong guy.
He was also tall, like the Horsehead.
He was tall.
Both these SS must've been ex-convicts.
They must have been criminals.
Because they looked like criminals, they acted like criminals.
And they-- and this is the type of people that they picked to deal with Jews.
To-- They were both beating me up.
And again-- I-- I had, already, a different shirt on-- then that shirt became bloody.
The entire shirt, all my clothing, were filled-- full of blood.
And he ordered-- this assistant commandant ordered the SS [?
Journe ?] that he should take me to the bunker.
What was the bunker?
The bunker was a cell in the ground in the camp.
That was the jail in the jail.
And no one came out from the bunker.
Because the bunker was extremely small.
It was just enough room for a person to stand.
It was a hole in the ground.
They gave down there a little soup.
And then, the next transport, they put that guy into the transport with a special notice that he should be shot on arrival.
That was the bunker.
He was taking me to the bunker.
The-- the personnel of the camp also consisted of some Jewish couples.
There was a Jewish-- what they called lagerfuhrer, a leader of the camp who was a Jew.
His name was Meyer.
He had-- [DRINKS] he had two assistants, one-- Jews.
One was Krauss.
And one was Foss.
As I mentioned before, they always picked German or Austrian Jews to-- to be the helpers, the kapos or whatsoever.
Because they could communicate with them.
I never met any of these people before.
The-- this Jewish lagerfuhrer, his name was Meyer.
And he was-- which I found out later, he was an opera singer from Vienna.
He was part Jew-- Jewish.
And that's why he was not deported.
Because, I think, one of his grandparents or two of his grandparents were Jewish.
The rest were not.
A very good looking man, very impressive, tall with a mustache.
And he assisted at the scene, when he read-- when the-- the assistant commander read the letter from the Gestapo.
And were they beating me up, and they were taking me to the bunker.
And somehow, he took pity on me.
And it was rare that he would intervene for somebody.
He kept to himself.
He wasn't bad.
He wasn't good.
But somehow, people told me that we're standing there-- I did not hear it, because I was already on my way to the bunker.
And he apparently said to the assistant commander, I don't think that this boy would have done something like that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And somehow, this was a miracle.
The-- the assistant commander told-- he-- he-- he screamed.
He called over to the SS that was taking me to the bunker.
I was very close to the bunker.
And he said, halt.
Don't take him to the bunker.
Instead of the bunker, tell him-- take him to the [GERMAN].
What was the [GERMAN]?
The [GERMAN] was one of those halls that they had for the prisoners.
And that was a place where the [GERMAN] were.
A [GERMAN] means, in German, somebody that tried to escape or escaped, an escapee.
A [GERMAN] is an escapee.
So if they caught an escapee, they put him in that place.
That was a separate place.
That was a place only for [GERMAN].
The procedure with [GERMAN] was they shaved their head, very close, like Yul Brynner.
And they put them on a red band.
And they stayed in that place.
And never in the history of the camp-- which was over three years-- was a [GERMAN] spared from being shipped out to Auschwitz.
I found out from the archives and Yad Vashem, from the that none-- no [GERMAN] is known to have survived the war.
And the director of Yad Vashem was extremely surprised when my daughter told him that her father is a [GERMAN] and he survived the war.
